rag.description: SendUser32KeyboardKey sends keyboard scan codes to native Windows applications via the User32 API. It supports single or multi-key sequences, sticky mode for simultaneous key combinations (shortcuts), configurable inter-key delays, and keyboard layout selection. Keys are specified by name using the Key parameter or directly as the Argument value. Requires a User32-compatible WebDriver and runs exclusively on Windows.

qa.question: What does SendUser32KeyboardKey do?
qa.answer: It sends one or more keyboard scan codes to a native Windows application using the User32 API, supporting sequential key presses, simultaneous shortcut combinations via sticky mode, configurable inter-key delays, and keyboard layout selection.

qa.question: What parameters does SendUser32KeyboardKey support?
qa.answer: Key (repeatable, specifies the key name to press), Delay (milliseconds between key inputs, default 0), Sticky (switch that holds all keys down simultaneously then releases them together), and KeyboardLayout (BCP-47 locale for scan code interpretation, default en-US).

qa.question: What is sticky mode and when should it be used?
qa.answer: Sticky mode is activated by the Sticky switch parameter. All specified keys are held down simultaneously and released together at the end, enabling keyboard shortcuts like Ctrl+C, Alt+F4, or Win+R that require concurrent key presses.

qa.question: What is the difference between sticky and non-sticky key sending?
qa.answer: Non-sticky mode presses each key independently in sequence with the configured delay between each press. Sticky mode holds all keys down simultaneously and releases them all at once, which is required for keyboard shortcuts that depend on modifier keys being held.

qa.question: How does the Key parameter interact with the Argument property?
qa.answer: The Key parameter is preferred when present. If Key is not specified as a parameter, the plugin falls back to the rule Argument value as the key to send. The Argument also serves as the carrier for the {{$ ...}} parameter expression syntax.

qa.question: What keyboard layouts are supported?
qa.answer: en-US (English United States, default) and he-IL (Hebrew Standard). The layout controls how key names are mapped to scan codes, which is important for locale-specific key behavior and multilingual automation.

qa.question: What happens when the WebDriver does not support IUser32Driver?
qa.answer: The plugin returns an empty response immediately and performs no keyboard action. This guard prevents execution in incompatible driver environments.

qa.question: How does the Delay parameter affect key input?
qa.answer: Delay specifies the pause in milliseconds between consecutive key inputs. It accepts a time string or a numeric millisecond value. Default is 0. Use it to simulate realistic typing cadence or to ensure the application processes each key before the next is sent.

qa.question: Can SendUser32KeyboardKey type text strings?
qa.answer: No. This plugin sends individual key scan codes. For typing text character-by-character, use the SendUser32Keys plugin instead.

description: The `SendUser32KeyboardKey` plugin simulates keyboard inputs using the Windows User32 API, targeting native application elements.
It is designed for scenarios where automation must interact with native or legacy Windows applications that lack standard WebDriver support.
| Native Application Support | Sends keyboard scan codes to native Windows application windows via the User32 API.                   |
| Multi-Key Sequences        | Supports repeating the `Key` parameter to build ordered sequences of key presses.                     |
| Sticky Mode                | Holds all specified keys down simultaneously and releases them together, enabling keyboard shortcuts. |
| Configurable Delay         | Introduces a per-key delay in milliseconds to simulate realistic interaction timing.                  |
| Keyboard Layout Selection  | Maps key names to scan codes using the specified locale, supporting multilingual keyboard inputs.     |
| Native App Automation | Enables RPA workflows to interact with native application UI via keyboard shortcuts, navigation, and commands. |
| Legacy Systems        | Facilitates automation of legacy systems that cannot be driven through standard WebDriver keyboard APIs.       |
| Application UI Testing  | Validates keyboard-driven interactions with native application elements using realistic OS-level key simulation.      |
| Comprehensive Scenarios | Expands test coverage to keyboard actions inaccessible through standard WebDriver, including shortcuts and modifiers. |

edge_cases: Empty or null Argument — key resolution falls back to an empty string and the server may reject the request
Non-IUser32Driver detected — plugin returns empty response with no key sent
Key name not recognized by the server — the server may raise an error or silently ignore the scan code
annotations.expected_result: The F5 key scan code is sent to the active native application window, triggering a refresh or other F5-bound action.
annotations.notes: When no parameter expression is used, the Argument value is treated directly as the single key name. This is the simplest form of the plugin requiring no parameter syntax.

description: ### Single key press via plain Argument
Send a single `F5` key press to the active native application window.
The key name is passed directly as the Argument value without any parameter expression, making this the simplest form of the plugin.

edge_cases: Both key names must be recognized — an unknown name causes a server-side error
Non-IUser32Driver detected — plugin returns empty response with no keys sent
Sticky mode with a single Key entry is valid but behaves identically to non-sticky
annotations.expected_result: Ctrl and C are held down simultaneously and then released together, sending the copy shortcut to the active native window.
annotations.notes: Sticky mode holds all specified keys down simultaneously and releases them at once. Use it for any shortcut that requires modifier keys to be held while pressing a regular key.

description: Simulate pressing `Ctrl + C` simultaneously using sticky mode to send the copy shortcut to the active native application.
The `--Sticky` switch holds all specified keys down at once and releases them together, which is required for modifier-key combinations.

edge_cases: Delay of 0 sends all keys back-to-back without any pause between them
Non-IUser32Driver detected — plugin returns empty response with no keys sent
Repeating the same key name multiple times is valid and each occurrence is sent as an individual press
annotations.expected_result: The Tab key is pressed three times in sequence with 300 ms between each press, advancing focus through three input fields.
annotations.notes: In non-sticky mode, each key is pressed and released individually in the order specified. Repeat --Key to build a sequence of any length.

description: ### Sequential Tab key presses with delay
Simulate pressing the `Tab` key three times in sequence with a 300-millisecond delay between each press to navigate through input fields in a native application form.
In non-sticky mode each key is pressed and released individually in the order specified.

edge_cases: Unsupported KeyboardLayout value — the server defaults to en-US or raises an error
Non-IUser32Driver detected — plugin returns empty response with no keys sent
The OS must have the he-IL keyboard layout installed for the layout switch to succeed
annotations.expected_result: Alt and Shift are held down simultaneously and released, triggering the keyboard layout switch to Hebrew Standard at the OS level.
annotations.notes: KeyboardLayout controls how key names are mapped to scan codes server-side. Specifying he-IL is necessary when the automation targets a Hebrew layout or must switch the OS layout using locale-aware scan codes.

description: ### Sticky layout switch — Alt+Shift with Hebrew keyboard layout
Simulate pressing `Alt + Shift` simultaneously using sticky mode to switch the OS keyboard layout to Hebrew Standard.
The `--KeyboardLayout:he-IL` parameter instructs the server to map key names using the Hebrew Standard scan code table.

edge_cases: All three key names must be recognized — an unknown name causes a server-side error
Non-IUser32Driver detected — plugin returns empty response with no keys sent
System-level shortcuts like Ctrl+Shift+Esc may be intercepted by the OS before reaching the target application
annotations.expected_result: Ctrl, Shift, and Esc are held down simultaneously and released together, opening the Windows Task Manager.
annotations.notes: Three-key sticky combinations follow the same pattern as two-key shortcuts. All keys are pressed simultaneously regardless of the number of Key parameters.

description: Simulate pressing `Ctrl + Shift + Esc` simultaneously using sticky mode to open the Windows Task Manager.
All three keys are held down at once and released together, which is required for this system-level keyboard shortcut.
Specifies the pause in milliseconds between consecutive key inputs during sequential (non-sticky) key sending.
Accepts a time string (e.g., `00:00:00.300`) or a numeric value representing milliseconds.
Default is `0`, meaning keys are sent back-to-back without any pause.
Use this parameter to simulate realistic human interaction cadence or to ensure the target application processes each key before the next one arrives.
parameters.name: Delay

description: Specifies the keyboard key to press, identified by its key name.
Repeat this parameter for each key in the sequence; each occurrence adds one key to the scan code list sent to the driver.
If not provided, the plugin falls back to the rule Argument value as the key source.
Use the values list to identify supported key names for the target keyboard layout.
parameters.name: Key

values.name: ArrowDown
description: Represents the Down arrow key (Arrow prefix form).

values.name: ArrowLeft
description: Represents the Left arrow key (Arrow prefix form).

values.name: ArrowRight
description: Represents the Right arrow key (Arrow prefix form).

values.name: ArrowUp
description: Represents the Up arrow key (Arrow prefix form).

values.name: Control
description: Represents the Control key (full-name form of Ctrl).

values.name: Shift
description: Represents the Shift key (generic, applies to either left or right Shift).
When present, enables sticky mode: all specified keys are pressed down simultaneously and released together at the end of the sequence.
Use sticky mode for keyboard shortcuts that require modifier keys to be held while pressing a regular key, such as `Ctrl+C`, `Alt+F4`, or `Win+R`.
Without this switch, each key is pressed and released individually in the order specified.
parameters.name: Sticky

description: Specifies the BCP-47 locale identifier used to map key names to OS-level scan codes.
Use `en-US` for standard English keyboard input and `he-IL` for Hebrew Standard keyboard layout.
The default layout is `en-US` when this parameter is omitted.
Specifying the correct layout is important when sending keys on non-English systems or when triggering OS layout-switch shortcuts.
parameters.name: KeyboardLayout

values.name: en-US
description: English (United States) keyboard layout. This is the default.
Carries the parameter expression or the direct key name to send via the User32 API.
Use the `{{$ --Key:Name}}` expression format to specify key names and options as runtime parameters.
When no parameter expression is used, the raw Argument value is treated as the single key name to press.
This property is the primary input for the plugin when the Key parameter is not specified inline.
properties.name: Argument

summary: The `SendUser32KeyboardKey` plugin simulates keyboard inputs using the Windows User32 API, targeting native application elements.
It supports sequential or simultaneous (sticky) key combinations, configurable inter-key delays, and keyboard layout selection for accurate scan code mapping.
